messages.content:
What type of suture is best to use in closing the deep layer?
proline 6-0
vicryl 4-0
nylon 5-0
silk 5-0
none of the above

None of the above. The choice of suture material and size to be used in closing the deep layer will depend on various factors like tissue type, tension on the wound, and the surgeon's preference. A surgeon may choose from a variety of absorbable and non-absorbable suture materials such as chromic gut, polyglactin, or polydioxanone based on these factors.
give examples of sutures best used for closing deep layer

1. Chromic gut: This is an absorbable suture material that can be used for closing the deep layer. It is made from sheep or cow intestines and is coated with chromium salt to delay absorption. Chromic gut is often used to close deep layers in the abdomen as it provides excellent tensile strength and resistance to absorption.

2. Polyglactin (Vicryl): This is an absorbable synthetic suture material that is commonly used for closing the deep layer. It is made of a copolymer of glycolide and lactide that provides excellent strength and knot security. The slow absorption rate allows it to maintain its strength for at least 2-3 weeks, giving sufficient time for healing.

3. Polydioxanone (PDS): This is an absorbable synthetic suture material that is ideal for closing the deep layer. It provides excellent tensile strength and holding power but unlike other absorbable sutures, it keeps its strength for up to six months after implantation.

4. Nylon: This is a non-absorbable suture material that can be used for closing the deep layer. It is made of synthetic polyamide fibers, which provide high tensile strength and excellent tissue compatibility.

5. Polypropylene: This is a non-absorbable suture material that is great for closing the deep layer. It is made of a synthetic polymer with excellent tensile strength and minimal tissue reactivity. Polypropylene sutures are inert and do not absorb, which may be useful in wounds where the tissue may take longer to heal.

Best suturing technique to use in closing the skin in this case?
simple continuous suturing
simple interrupted suturing
horizontal mattress
vertical mattress
interlocking suturing
subcuticular suturing

The choice of suturing technique used to close the skin will depend on various factors such as the wound's location, size, and tension. However, the most appropriate technique for closing the skin for this case would be a subcuticular suture technique.

The subcuticular suture is a continuous suturing technique that is placed in the dermal layer, just below the skin surface. This technique involves closing the skin edges with a series of small, closely placed stitches without puncturing the skin surface, which reduces the risk of scarring and infection.

This technique is highly recommended for wounds in areas of the body where the cosmetic outcome is critical, such as the face, neck, and extremities. It provides excellent eversion and wound edge alignment, which helps to minimize tension, thus reducing the risk of dehiscence and scarring.

Other suturing techniques like the simple continuous, simple interrupted, horizontal mattress, vertical mattress, and interlocking suturing can also be considered depending on the wound's location, size, and tension.

A patient came to the ER with a 3 cm lacerated wound at the right 
malar area due to motor vehicular crash. All vital signs are normal, 
no history of loss of consciousness and with normal neurologic 
exam findings. No facial bony deformation noted.

Best suturing technique to use in closing the skin in this case?

For this particular case, the best suturing technique to use in closing the skin would be a simple interrupted suturing technique.

The simple interrupted suture technique is the most commonly used technique for closing skin lacerations or wounds. It involves placing individual sutures across the wound edges and tying each stitch in a knot. This technique provides good wound edge eversion and alignment, and it can be used for wounds of any size and shape.

For a 3 cm lacerated wound at the right malar area, a simple interrupted suture technique would be sufficient to achieve good cosmetic results. However, the choice of suturing technique may also depend on the location and tension of the wound, as well as the surgeon's preference. Additionally, wound infection prophylaxis using appropriate antibiotics may be considered, depending on the patient's risk factors.

in this case, when would be the best time to remove the skin 
sutures?
third day post repair
ten to fourteen days post repair
five to seven days post repair
twenty-one days post repair

The best time to remove skin sutures for a 3 cm lacerated wound at the right malar area would be five to seven days post repair.

The timing of skin suture removal depends on several factors, including the location of the wound, the type of suture material used, and the extent of healing. In general, skin sutures can be removed when the wound has reached sufficient strength to support normal activity, and the risk of dehiscence or wound opening is minimal.

For most facial wounds, skin sutures can be safely removed between 5 to 7 days after repair. At this time, the wound should have sufficient strength to support normal facial movements, and there should be no more than minimal risk of wound opening or dehiscence. Delaying the suture removal beyond 7 days may increase the risk of scarring and infection, while removing the sutures too early may lead to wound opening and delayed healing.

However, the timing of suture removal is ultimately up to the surgeon's judgment and may vary depending on the individual patient's condition and the extent of wound healing.

After surgery of a SCCA involving the gingiva with extension to the mandible and skin resulted in a large thru & thru defect with resection of the mandibular body on the right. Which of the following regional flap is best suited to reconstruct this said defect?
deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap
pectoralis major myocutaneous flap
trapezius myocutaneous flap
pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap
trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap
pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and deltopectoral 
fasciocutaneous flap

For the reconstruction of a large thru & thru defect with resection of the mandibular body on the right following surgery of a SCCA involving the gingiva with extension to the mandible and skin, the best regional flap that is suited for the reconstruction would be the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap.

The pectoralis major myocutaneous flap is a reliable workhorse for reconstructing large defects of the head and neck, especially when there is a need for donor tissue that is well vascularized, has a large volume, and reliable. It is a composite flap that includes the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and the pectoralis major muscle. The flap is based on the thoracoacromial artery and its branches, which provides a robust blood supply to the flap.

In this particular case, the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap is best suited for reconstructing the large thru & thru defect that involves the mandible and skin. Other regional flaps like the deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap, trapezius myocutaneous flap, pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap, trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap, or the pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap may not be the ideal option due to the extent of the defect and the need for a large volume of well-vascularized donor tissue.

A Basal Cell CA was excised on the right maxillary area resulting to a 6 cm defect. To reconstruct, which of the following will be the best to use?
DP fasciocutaneous flap
Pectoralis major myocutaneous flap
Trapezius myocutaneous flap
pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap
trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap
pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and DP fasciocutaneous flaps

For the reconstruction of a 6 cm defect resulting from the excision of a Basal Cell CA on the right maxillary area, the best option would depend on various factors such as the location and size of the defect, the availability of donor tissue and the surgeon's preference. However, of the flaps mentioned, the most appropriate option would be the deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap.

The deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap is a reliable and versatile option for reconstructing defects in the head and neck. It provides an abundant and well-vascularized donor tissue that can be used for coverage of large defects of the maxillary region. The skin and subcutaneous tissue of the flap are raised based on the thoracoacromial artery and vein, while the deltoid muscle serves as a myocutaneous extension. 

Pectoralis Major Myocutaneous Flap and Trapezius Myocutaneous Flap may not be the ideal options for the reconstruction of a 6 cm maxillary defect as they are usually reserved for more extensive defects which need larger volume of tissue reconstruction.

Pectoralis Major Osteomyocutaneous Flap, Trapezius Osteomyocutaneous Flap, and Pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flaps are usually saved for more extensive defects or reconstructions that require both soft tissue and bony replacement.

In summary, for the reconstruction of a 6 cm defect resulting from a Basal Cell CA excision on the right maxillary area, the deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap would be a reliable and appropriate option.

A Parotid malignancy involving the skin was surgically removed resulting in a large skin defect and exposure of the mandible. Which of the following regional flaps is best to use in the reconstruction? give the choice
DP fasciocutaneous flap
Pectoralis major myocutaneous flap
Trapezius myocutaneous flap
pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap
trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap
pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and DP fasciocutaneous flaps

For the reconstruction of a large skin defect and exposure of the mandible after surgical removal of a Parotid malignancy involving the skin, the best regional flap that is suited for the reconstruction would be the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap.

The pectoralis major myocutaneous flap is a reliable choice for head and neck reconstruction after major ablative surgery. It is based on the thoracoacromial artery and consists of a skin paddle, the pectoral major muscle, and a segment of the pectoralis minor muscle, providing a well-vascularized bulk of tissue that is robust enough to withstand infections.

In this particular case, the extent of the defect and the exposure of the mandible indicates a need for a robust and versatile flap that can provide ample tissue coverage with strong vascularity. The pectoralis major flap is ideal for this purpose and can provide sufficient tissue coverage to restore the skin defect while covering the exposed bone. 

Other regional flaps like the deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap, trapezius myocutaneous flap, pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap, trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap, or the pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap may not be the ideal option due to the extent of the defect and the need for a large volume of well-vascularized donor tissue. Additionally, the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap provides the added benefit of being able to obliterate the dead space and fill the defect.

In summary, the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap would be the best option for the reconstruction of a large skin defect with exposure of the mandible after surgical removal of a parotid malignancy involving the skin.

What is the vascular supply of Pectoralis major myocutaneous flap?
a branch of the internal mammary artery
a branch of the thyrocervical artery
a branch of the thoracoacromial artery
a branch of the transverse cervical artery

The vascular supply of the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap is a branch of the thoracoacromial artery.

The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the axillary artery and arises from the second part of the axillary artery in the axilla. It runs across the clavicle and divides into four branches, including the pectoral branch, which gives rise to the blood supply to the pectoralis major muscle and the overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue that make up the pectoralis major myocutaneous flap. 

This robust blood supply provides a well-vascularized bulk of tissue that can be used for head and neck reconstruction, including defects in the oral cavity, pharynx, and larynx. Additionally, the addition of the pectoralis minor muscle and the accessory thoracoacromial vessels to this flap further ensures its robustness and reliability.
